paper_title: Value-Aware Stochastic KV Cache Eviction for Reasoning Models

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Reasoning models improve accuracy through extended chains of thought, but their long outputs create a memory and compute bottleneck. KV cache eviction methods reduce this cost by evicting unimportant key-value pairs from the cache, yet they often yield worse accuracy than selection-based sparse attention alternatives, which keep the full KV cache. We identify key factors crucial to KV cache eviction accuracy. First, a small fraction of value states have abnormally large magnitudes, and evicting them causes catastrophic failure where models enter repetitive reasoning loops. Second, introducing stochasticity during eviction improves accuracy by increasing cache diversity. Based on these findings, we propose Value-aware Stochastic KV Cache Eviction (VaSE), a training-free recipe that protects large-magnitude value states and promotes diverse eviction decisions. Across six reasoning tasks, Qwen3 models using VaSE with 4x KV cache compression yield higher average accuracies than SOTA selection method at the same sparsity, while outperforming the strongest eviction method by more than 4%. Overall, VaSE bridges the gap between efficiency and accuracy, supporting FlashAttention2 and enabling a static memory footprint for reasoning models.

sections.content: Value-Aware Stochastic KV Cache Eviction for Reasoning Models Ting-Yun Changσ Harvey Yiyun Fuχ Deqing Fuσ Chenghao Yangχ Jesse Thomasonσ Robin Jiaσ σUniversity of Southern California χUniversity of Chicago 6 2 0 2 J 2 ] . [ 1 8 2 9 3 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r {tingyun,deqingfu,jessetho,robinjia}@usc.edu {harveyfu,chenghao}@uchicago.edu

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Reasoning models improve accuracy through extended chains of thought, but their long outputs create memory and compute bottleneck. KV cache eviction methods reduce this cost by evicting unimportant key-value pairs from the cache, yet they often yield worse accuracy than selection-based sparse attention alternatives, which keep the full KV cache. We identify key factors crucial to KV cache eviction accuracy. First, small fraction of value states have abnormally large magnitudes, and evicting them causes catastrophic failure where models enter repetitive reasoning loops. Second, introducing stochasticity during eviction improves accuracy by increasing cache diversity. Based on these findings, we propose Value-aware Stochastic KV Cache Eviction (VASE), training-free recipe that protects large-magnitude value states and promotes diverse eviction decisions. Across six reasoning tasks, Qwen3 models using VASE with 4 KV cache compression yield higher average accuracies than SOTA selection method at the same sparsity, while outperforming the strongest eviction method by more than 4%. Overall, VASE bridges the gap between efficiency and accuracy, supporting FlashAttention2 and enabling static memory footprint for reasoning models.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Reasoning models (OpenAI, 2024; Qwen, 2024; Guo et al., 2025a) leverage test-time compute (Snell et al., 2025) to improve accuracy by generating extended chains of thought before producing final answer. While this approach yields high accuracy on complex tasks, it introduces an efficiency bottleneck at test time because the reasoning models tend to generate unnecessary long outputs. For example, Chen et al. (2025) show that models may overthink simple arithmetic question, using more than 900 tokens to answer 2 + 3 = ?. As consequence, an auto-regressive large language model that stores the key-value representations of every past token incurs substantial memory and computational overhead as the sequence length grows. Sparse attention (Zhao et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020; Kitaev et al., 2020) addresses this bottleneck by attending to only subset of previous tokens; such approaches fall into two broad categories. KV cache selection methods (Ribar et al., 2024; Tang et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2025b; Gao et al., 2026) keep the full KV cache in memory but activate only sparse subset of KV pairs at each decode step. These methods reduce compute and memory movement, but their memory footprints scale linearly with sequence length. In contrast, KV cache eviction methods (Zhang et al., 2023; Ge et al., 2024; Devoto et al., 2024; Oren et al., 2024) permanently discard low-importance KV pairs once the cache reaches predefined budget. While eviction methods (Li et al., 2024; Cai et al., 2025) suffer larger accuracy degradation on reasoning tasks compared to their selection-based counterparts, they can yield static memory footprint and better throughput. Equal contribution. Figure 1: Top: VASE is KV Cache eviction method that combines stochastic sampling with valuestate magnitude scoring to retain diverse and important KV pairs under fixed KV cache budget. Bottom: By integrating both stochasticity and value awareness, VASE outperforms baseline methods that use either signal alone, improving average pass@1 accuracy across various reasoning tasks. In this paper, we reveal two key findings to improve KV cache eviction methods on reasoning models. First, the magnitudes of value states show strongly skewed distributions, with small fraction of tokens having abnormally large vector magnitudes (see Figure 2). Those large-magnitude value states are crucial for maintaining task accuracies: evicting them causes the models to enter repetitive loops, where they endlessly re-examine the context or generate nonsensical outputs without reaching final answer (see Figure 9 for the examples). Second, we find that introducing stochasticity during KV cache eviction effectively improves reasoning task accuracies. We attribute this to improved diversity of the retained KV pairs, which together yield more representative coverage of the full context. Our case study on GSM8K demonstrates that incorporating value scoring boosts Qwen3-4Bs accuracy by as much as 16.2%, while introducing stochasticity yields an additional 4.7% improvement. Building on these findings, we propose Value-aware Stochastic KV Cache Eviction (VASE), training-free KV cache eviction recipe that prioritizes large-magnitude value states and introduces stochasticity during eviction. We target the decoding step of reasoning models, by applying VASE to Qwen3 models (Yang et al., 2025a) and evaluating on six tasks spanning math, code generation, and science question answering. On average, VASE outperforms the previously SOTA eviction method R-KV (Cai et al., 2025) by 4.4% on Qwen3-4B and 4.9% on Qwen3-14B. When combined with CurDKV (Sengupta et al., 2025), stochastic sampling further boosts its average accuracy by 7.7% and 9.2% on the two models, respectively. VASE even slightly surpasses the average accuracy of SeerAttention-R (Gao et al., 2026), strong selection method whose KV cache memory grows linearly with the sequence length, on both models while using static memory. In addition, we benchmark the actual throughput and peak memory of different methods, showing that VASE achieves higher throughput and lower memory footprint than the R-KV eviction baseline across token budgets. Our results match closely with the theoretical memory compression ratio. Furthermore, our insights generalize to other efficiency methods like KV cache quantization; we show that large-magnitude value states also lead to disproportionately large reconstruction errors under per-token quantization (Liu et al., 2024b). Together, we demonstrate that value-state magnitude and stochasticity are fundamental axes of KV cache management, with our VASE recipe bridging the efficiency-accuracy gap left by prior sparse attention methods.1 1Our code is available at https://github.com/terarachang/VaSE.

sections.title: 2 Problem Setup
sections.content: We formalize the decode-phase KV cache eviction setting by drawing the distinction between selection-based and eviction-based methods, and introduce several representative eviction baselines. Selection-based method. Selection-based sparse attention methods retain the full KV cache but activate only sparse subset of KV pairs at each decoding step. representative example is SeerAttention-R (Gao et al., 2026), which uses trained attention gate to predict attention sparsity patterns and selectively activates different KV cache blocks during inference, thereby reducing computational complexity and memory movement. Compared to eviction-based methods, SeerAttention-R retains all KV pairs and the memory footprint scales linearly as O(T ) with sequence length . Eviction-based method. KV cache eviction methods permanently discard low-importance keyvalue pairs once the cache size reaches predefined budget, reducing memory cost at the risk of irrecoverable information loss. We specifically focus on eviction methods for the decoding step of autoregressive reasoning models, as these models can generate over 10,000 tokens for math question that has fewer than 200 prompt tokens (see Table 4 for statistics). Formally, let be the total KV cache budget, and be the dimension of keys and values. As the KV cache budget fills up at the token index t, we have KV pairs (ki, vi) for = 1, . . . , , where ki Rd, vi Rd. Given the current query qt Rd, the attention head computes its output as weighted sum over the cached values, ot = (cid:88) iN α(t) vi, α(t) = (cid:16) exp ki/ (cid:16) (cid:17) (cid:80) jN exp kj/ (cid:17) , (1) where α(t) products between the current query and all cached keys (Vaswani et al., 2017). is the attention weight assigned to token i, obtained by applying softmax to the scaled dot Periodic eviction with budget and buffer B. We adopt the periodic-eviction framework proposed by Cai et al. (2025) and Song et al. (2025), which targets decode-phase compression. The framework consists of persistent budget of tokens and buffer of size holding the most recent tokens, capping the total cache size at = + tokens. Since each decoding step adds one KV pair to the cache, the buffer fills every steps and triggers an eviction step: the eviction operator chooses pairs to discard, clears the buffer, and restores the cache to size (See the upper-left panel in Figure 1). Concretely, the most recent KV pairs in the buffer are protected from eviction. scoring function then assigns scalar importance score to each of the remaining candidates, and the candidates with the lowest importance scores will be discarded. Below, we describe the scoring functions of three prior eviction methods, SnapKV (Li et al., 2024), R-KV (Cai et al., 2025), and CurDKV(Sengupta et al., 2025). Following common practice, KV pairs are scored independently for each attention head and layer. To simplify notation, we omit layer and head indices hereafter. SnapKV. Li et al. (2024) rank candidate pairs by their average attention score over window of recent queries, where we set the window size to be the same as the buffer size B.2 αi := 1 (cid:88) α(τ ) , τ =tB+1 (2) where is the KV pair index, is the eviction timestep, and α(τ ) is the attention score defined in Eq. 1. SnapKV uses keys for the attention computation, but does not consider values in the scoring function. R-KV. Cai et al. (2025) specifically target redundant tokens in reasoning models. They augment the SnapKV score αi with redundancy penalty ri to promote diversity among retained KV pairs: sRKV := λ αi (1 λ) ri, where ri = Softmax CosSim(ki, kj) , (3) 1 (cid:88) j=i where λ [0, 1] is hyperparameter. high ri indicates that token has high cosine similarity to many cached tokens; subtracting it discourages choosing redundant pairs. 2Following Li et al. (2024), we apply average pooling to αi to smooth the attention scores. Following the decode-phase SnapKV variant introduced by Cai et al. (2025), we cache the most recent queries and compute non-causal attention scores. 3 Figure 2: Left: Range distribution of the value states. The violin plots show the presence of extreme magnitude outliers at different layers. Right: Evicting the large magnitude outliers causes accuracy to collapse to 14.3%, greatly underperforming random eviction baseline at the same token budget, suggesting that these large-magnitude value states are crucial to model accuracy. CurDKV. Sengupta et al. (2025) observe the limitations of scoring KV pairs with attention alone, which ignores value states. They compute leverage scores for keys and values respectively via approximate CUR matrix decomposition and combine them into single importance score. Originally designed for the prefill stage, we extend CurDKV for the decode phase with the periodic eviction framework. Specifically, CurDKV draws Gaussian projection Rdr at the start of generation, with each entry Gab iid (0, 1/r), and rank candidates by the product of key and value scores: sCUR := ℓ(K) (G) ℓ(V ) (G), where ℓ(K) (G) := Gki2 2, ℓ(V ) (G) := Gvi 2. (4) CurDKV considers value states during scoring, but it does not promote diversity among KV pairs.

sections.title: 3 Methodology
sections.content: We hypothesize that incorporating value states and diversity into KV cache scoring is essential for preserving model accuracy under eviction. Inspired by the scoring function of CurDKV, which favors value vectors with larger magnitudes, and by the finding of Sun et al. (2024) that massive activations in LLMs are indispensable, we first study the effect of large-magnitude value states on eviction accuracy. Based on the analysis, we propose framework that prioritizes those large-magnitude value states within the scoring function and introduces stochasticity to promote diversity. 3.1 Significance of Value States with Large Magnitudes We first analyze the distribution of value-state magnitudes, where we extract the full KV cache from Qwen3-4B while generating responses to GSM8K examples (Cobbe et al., 2021). We define the magnitude3 of cached value vector Rd as: Range(v) := max j[d] vj min j[d] vj (5) where vj is the j-th entry of v, and Range(v) is chosen over L2(v) for its connection to quantization (see 4.3). Figure 2 shows that the distribution of Range(v) has large outliers over layers (see Appendix for more layers). We hypothesize that these large-Range outliers are critical to the model accuracy. Because attention output is computed as weighted summation of values (Eq. 1), those values with larger magnitudes have disproportionate influence on the output. To validate this hypothesis, we purposefully evict the largest KV pairs ranked by Range(vi). This reduces GSM8K accuracy to just 14.3%, under-performing random eviction by 38.9% at the same 512-token budget (Figure 2; Right). Further inspection of the model outputs reveals that the model tends to enter endless loops of self-reflection and never reaches conclusion; it also tends to generate non-grammatical sentences (see Figure 9). Prior work (Guo et al., 2024, 2025b) observes that low-magnitude values co-occur with attention sinks (Xiao et al., 2024), which are crucial to 3We experiment with three variants, Range, L2 norm, and variance, to capture the magnitude and variety of value vectors in Appendix A. We find that these variants are positively correlated and lead to comparable accuracies. Method Family Key score Value score Diversity SeerAttention-R (Gao et al., 2026) Select Attn Gate SnapKV (Li et al., 2024) R-KV (Cai et al., 2025) CurDKV (Sengupta et al., 2025) VASE-ATTNV(ours) VASE-DKV(ours) Evict Evict Evict Evict Evict αi λ αi (1 λ) ri ℓ(K) = (KG)i2 2 ℓ(V ) = (V G)i2 2 redundancy ri αi ℓ(K) i,t = (KGt)i2 Range(vi) ℓ(V ) i,t = (V Gt)i2 2 sample αi sample Gt Table 1: Design space of sparse attention methods. Among eviction methods, prior work covers at most two of the three axes (key-based scoring, value-based scoring, diversity); VASE is the first to combine all three, instantiated as VASE-AttnV and VASE-DKV. eviction methods. In this paper, we demonstrate that large-magnitude values are equally important and should be up-weighted within the scoring function (4.1). 3.2 VASE: Value-Aware Stochastic Eviction We propose VASE (Value-aware Stochastic Eviction), training-free eviction recipe designed around two principles: upweighting large-magnitude value states in the scoring function, and introducing stochasticity to promote diversity in the retained cache. We propose two VASE variants that apply our recipe to SnapKV and CurDKV, respectively. VASE-ATTNV. VASE-ATTNV reserves part of the token budget for large-magnitude value states and applies stochastic sampling on top of SnapKV. Recall that is the persistent budget and is the buffer size ( 2). We first rank each KV pair by Range(vi) (Eq. 5), where [K] indexes the non-buffer tokens. Given value reservation budget Nv < K, we select the Nv candidates with the largest Range(vi) and unconditionally retain them as the reserved set RV . We denote the set of tokens in the buffer as RB, forming the full reserved set = RV RB. The remaining slots are then filled by sampling from the SnapKV attention distribution, with weights proportional to αi (Eq. 2). This preserves SnapKVs core idea of favoring high-attention tokens, while replacing its hard topk selection with soft, probabilistic one. Stochastic sampling offers key advantage over topk in terms of token retention flexibility. Let π(t) denote the probability of token being retained at step t. After eviction steps, the probability that token is kept in the KV cache can be factorized as (cid:81)T . Under topk, any token below the cutoff is evicted with certainty (π(t) = 0). In contrast, under sampling, every factor is strictly positive, π(t) 1 (1 α(t) is the candidate set for sampling; therefore, each token has nonzero probability of being retained across all steps. /Zt)KR > 0, where Zt = (cid:80) and Ct = [K] R(t) t=1 π(t) α(t) jCt i VASE-DKV. VASE-DKV inherits value-state awareness directly from CurDKVs product score (Eq. 4), where the value score ℓ(V ) (G) upweights large-magnitude values. On top of this, we introduce stochasticity through the Gaussian matrix at each eviction step to promote diversity among the retained KV pairs. Specifically, at step we resample random projection Gt Rdr independently of all past projections, and compute the leverage scores following Eq. 4. This improves upon CurDKV, which uses fixed across all steps: tokens with certain representations are then consistently assigned low scores and deterministically evicted. Resampling avoids this failure mode by applying an independent scoring criterion at each eviction step such that no token would be permanently disadvantaged by single projection.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: 4.1 Isolating the Effects of Value-Awareness and Stochasticity We perform case study on GSM8K to isolate the individual contributions of value-awareness and stochasticity to the accuracy of Qwen3-4B. 5 Figure 3: Accuracy of Qwen3-4B on GSM8K with KV cache budget of K=512 (4 compression). +V16 indicates reserving 16 slots in the budget for value scoring. By combining stochastic sampling with value scoring, our methods achieve accuracy close to the full model (88.4%). Value Awareness. We experiment with different reservation budgets Nv to evaluate the efficacy of our value-scoring function Range(v) (Eq. 5). To isolate the effect of our value scoring, we implement VASE-ATTNVwithout sampling. Specifically, we fill the remaining budget with KV pairs ranked highest by SnapKVs scoring function αi (Eq. 2). Figure 3 shows that when increasing the number of Nv from 16 to 256 (green bars), the accuracies on GSM8K increase consistently from 73.3%(+9.0%) to 80.5%(+16.2%), showing the importance of keeping large-range values from eviction. Stochasticity. We introduce stochasticity into SnapKV and CurDKV to promote diversity in KV pair retention. For SnapKV (leftmost in Figure 3), replacing its deterministic topk selection with αi-weighted sampling improves model accuracy from 64.3% to 70.9%. Similarly, for CurDKV (rightmost), resampling Gaussian matrix Gt at each eviction step throughout generation improves accuracy from 78.6% to 87.6%. The teal bars show that value scoring and stochasticity are complementary, with the combination of both strategies achieving the highest accuracies. 4.2 Main Results Setup. We evaluate VASE on Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-14B (Yang et al., 2025a) across six reasoning tasks: AIME25, AIME26 (Art of Problem Solving), HMMT25 (Feb and Nov splits combined; HMMT 2025), GPQA-Diamond (Rein et al., 2024), MATH (Hendrycks et al., 2021), and LiveCodeBench-v6 (Jain et al., 2025).4 All eviction methods operate under the periodic-eviction framework ( 2) with shared recency buffer of = 64 and persistent budget K. For each task, we first measure the average number of tokens Navg under the uncompressed full model and then set the eviction budget to approximately Navg/4, where {1024, 2048, 4096}. This yields nominal 4 compression ratio over the uncompressed baseline. SeerAttention-R is configured at the matching 25% activation ratio so that all sparse methods operate at the same effective attention sparsity. We use the models default top-p configurations for all generations. We report pass@1 results, which are averaged over 16 runs for datasets under 100 examples, and 8 runs for larger datasets. We select the hyperparameters on GSM8K for each method and apply them to other tasks without further tuning (see Appendix B). VASE-ATTNV achieves the best average accuracy. Table 2 shows that both VASE variants outperform every prior eviction baseline on the per-model average and reach parity with the selection method SeerAttention-R. On Qwen3-4B, VASE-ATTNV achieves an average accuracy of 59.09%, edging out SeerAttention-R (58.81%) and surpassing the strongest eviction baseline R-KV (54.69%) by 4.4%; VASE-DKV reaches 57.48%, improving over CurDKV (49.78%) by 7.7%. The same pattern holds on Qwen3-14B: VASE-ATTNV (65.81%) matches SeerAttention-R (65.37%) and outperforms R-KV (60.90%) by 4.9%; VASE-DKV (64.94%) improves over the deterministic CurDKV baseline (55.70%) by 9.2%. Overall, VASE-ATTNV achieves the best average accuracy among all methods. While SnapKV and R-KV baselines do not consider value states in their deterministic scoring, the results of VASE-ATTNV show the effectiveness of combining value awareness with stochasticity in the eviction scoring function. The substantial improvements of VASE-DKV over CurDKV further highlight the importance of stochasticity. Since both variants 4We use the same evaluation scripts as SeerAttention-R: https://github.com/microsoft/SeerAttention 6 Model Method AIME25 AIME26 HMMT25 GPQA-D MATH Avg. Qwen3-4B Qwen3-14B Full SeerAttention-R SnapKV R-KV CurDKV VASE-DKV VASE-ATTNV Full SeerAttention-R R-KV CurDKV VASE-DKV VASE-ATTNV 66.41 58.59 47.92 49.58 48.75 57.29 59.17 70.21 64.79 54.58 53.75 63.33 63.96 62.71 60.42 53.33 54.58 54.58 61.88 62.08 76.04 65.62 60.21 61.25 68.75 72. 45.94 40.42 35.31 36.98 33.23 39.58 44.38 54.79 48.65 44.58 39.58 49.38 50.00 56.19 49.94 36.87 49.05 37.94 43.94 47.98 65.25 61.68 59.09 48.48 56.76 57.39 93.93 84.67 72.30 83.28 74.42 84.72 81.82 95.22 86.12 86.05 75.42 86.47 85. 65.04 58.81 49.15 54.69 49.78 57.48 59.09 72.30 65.37 60.90 55.70 64.94 65.81 Table 2: Reasoning-task accuracy (%) of sparse attention methods on Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-14B with 25% of full KV activated (4 compression for eviction methods). Bold marks the best eviction method of each task. Our eviction methods VASE achieve comparable average accuracy to SeerAttention-R, the SOTA selection method. of VASE belong to the eviction family, we demonstrate that the VASE recipe can recover accuracy without paying the memory cost of selection methods. Code generation. Figure 5 (Left) reports pass@1 of Qwen3-4B on LiveCodeBench-v6-Medium under 2048-token budget ( 20% of full KV size). R-KV (62.6%), VASE-DKV (61.9%), and VASEATTNV (63.5%) achieve comparable performance, while CurDKV only yields 34.6%. Surprisingly, SeerAttention-R (45.3%) underperforms eviction methods, with the exception of CurDKV. This is likely because the learning-based attention gate of SeerAttention-R struggles to generalize under domain shifts, while the training-free eviction methods are less affected. Token Budgets. Figure 4 reports pass@1 accuracy of Qwen3-14B on AIME26 and HMMT25 as the KV cache budget increases from 2048 to 6144 tokens. At the tightest budget of 2048 tokens, which corresponds to roughly 7.5 compression of the full cache on AIME26 and 8.7 on HMMT25, both VASE variants show clear improvement over R-KV and CurDKV baselines. As the budget increases, the performance gap diminishes with the accuracy of all methods recovering. Nevertheless, VASE methods (teal lines) consistently outperform the baselines. We observe this pattern in both datasets: the accuracy gains from value awareness and stochastic eviction are largest under aggressive compression and diminish gracefully when the cache capacity is abundant (see Table 6 in the appendix for results on GPQA-Diamond). 4.3 Large-Range Values Correlate with KV Cache Quantization Error Our findings regarding large-Range values have deep connection with per-token KV cache quantization (Liu et al., 2024b; Su and Yuan, 2025), an alternative way to compress the KV cache. Formally, in asymmetric b-bit linear quantization, the range of the input value state v, [min v, max v], is mapped to the full range of the quantized integer space, [0, 2b 1]. The b-bit integer quantization-dequantization process can be expressed as: Q(v) = (cid:22) zv sv (cid:25) , = Q(v) sv + zv, (6) where zv = min is the zero point, sv = (max min v)/(2b 1) = Range(v)/(2b 1) is the scaling factor, is the rounding operation, and is the reconstructed vector of after dequantization. The scaling factor sv represents the step size of quantization: if Range(v) is small, sv is small, leading to fine-grained quantization, and the reconstructed value is close to the original value v; on the other hand, if Range(v) is large, large sv results in large and coarse step size, causing to deviate significantly from v, which is known as the outlier issue (Dettmers et al., 2022) in LLM quantization. 7 Figure 4: Pass@1 accuracy of Qwen3-14B under varying KV cache budgets. The full-cache model uses an average of 15.3k and 17.8k tokens on AIME26 and HMMT25, respectively. Each x-axis label reports the number of budget tokens (e.g., 2048) and its corresponding KV cache compression ratio to the full model (e.g., 7.5). Figure 5: Left: Pass@1 results of Qwen3-4B on LiveCodeBench under 2048-token budget (20% of full KV). R-KV and our VASE methods achieve the strongest performance. Right: Range(v) and per-token value cache quantization errors are highly correlated under different quantization configurations, where b2g32 means 2-bit precision with group size of 32. We validate the relationship between Range(v) and per-token quantization error on the value cache empirically, using the HQQ quantizer (Badri and Shaji, 2023) with different bit-widths and quantization group sizes. Quantization error is measured via the mean-squared reconstruction error between and (Frantar et al., 2023). We extract the full value cache from Qwen3-4B during the generation of GSM8K examples, quantize them, and compute the per-token mean-squared error. Figure 5 (Right) demonstrates that Range(v) and the reconstruction error are highly correlated across layers under different quantization configurations, suggesting that large-Range value states lead to large information loss under per-token KV cache quantization.

sections.title: 5 Benchmark Throughput and Memory
sections.content: In this section, we benchmark the actual decode-phase throughput and peak memory of different methods on the Qwen3-14B model using single A100-80G GPU. We sweep over KV cache budgets {2048, 4096, 6144} and total output tokens {16384, 32768}. All methods are benchmarked with FlashAttention2 kernels (Dao, 2024), without PagedAttention (Kwon et al., 2023). Our benchmark script builds on that of Song et al. (2025); we set the batch size to 16 and the input prompt length to 256 tokens across all experiments. Figure 6 (Left & Middle) shows that all eviction methods achieve substantially higher throughput than the Full baseline, with consistent ordering of VASE-DKV> VASE-ATTNV> R-KV> Full across all settings. For example, at 16K output tokens and KV cache budget of 2048, VASE-DKV is 3.1 faster than Full (411 vs. 133 tokens per second). Our VASE-DKV is the fastest eviction method because it does not compute attention scores during eviction, and VASE-ATTNV outperforms 8 Figure 6: Left & Middle: Decode throughput () of the Qwen3-14B model on single A100-80G GPU under different KV cache budgets {2048, 4096, 6144} and total output tokens {16K, 32K}. All eviction methods run well above the original Full method (dashed line; OOM at 32K), with VASE-DKV achieving the fastest throughput. Right: Peak GPU memory () at the 16K output tokens and 4096 budget; the 14B model weights (hatched) account for nearly 30GB of memory. All eviction methods have much lower memory footprint than Full, with VASE-DKV using the least memory. R-KV because it avoids the additional redundancy-score computation used to promote diversity. For all eviction methods, the throughput decreases with increase of the token budget. On the other hand, the ordering of memory footprint (Figure 6, Right, ) is reversed: VASE-DKV< VASE-ATTNV< R-KV< Full. Figure 11 in the appendix shows consistent ranking under different token budgets. Excluding the memory of the 14B model weights (the hatched bars), our VASE methods achieve roughly 4 theoretical KV cache compression (16K/4K) over the Full baseline.

sections.title: 6 Related Work
sections.content: KV Cache Compression Methods. Prior work reduces the cost of the KV cache through different approaches: reducing the number of tokens via sparse attention (Liu et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2024; Singhania et al., 2024; Chari and Van Durme, 2025), reducing the precision via quantization (Hooper et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024b; Kim et al., 2025; Zandieh et al., 2026), and reducing the hidden dimension via low-rank decomposition (Saxena et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024a; Chang et al., 2025). In this paper, we focus on sparse attention methods. Our findings on the importance of large-magnitude values also carry profound implications for per-token KV cache quantization (Liu et al., 2024b). Decoding Phase Compression. Inference proceeds in two phases. In the prefill phase, the model processes the -token input prompt and computes keyvalue pairs in parallel. In the decode phase, the model generates one token at time and appends the corresponding KV pairs to the cache. Unlike the prefill phase, the decode phase is memory-bound, and the growing cache intensifies the memory bottleneck. For reasoning models, the KV pairs from the decode phase dominate the cost due to the long thinking traces. Therefore, several recent works (Cai et al., 2025; Song et al., 2025; Gao et al., 2026; Guo et al., 2026) have targeted KV cache compression during the decoding phase of reasoning models. Our paper also focuses on the decoding phase compression of reasoning models. Unlike Song et al. (2025), we do not distinguish between prompt tokens and generated tokens at the eviction step; therefore, prompt tokens may also be evicted in our experiments. Leveraging Value-State Magnitude for KV Cache Scoring. Typically, sparse attention involves scoring key-value pairs by their importance. Because the output of attention is weighted combination of values, Guo et al. (2024); Devoto et al. (2025) score KV pairs by weighting attention scores with the norm of their corresponding values. In this paper, we deeply investigate the importance of largemagnitude values. Rather than multiplying attention and value scores, we propose an alternative, VASE-ATTNV, which reserves dedicated slots in the token budget to ensure that large-magnitude values are always preserved in the cache.

sections.title: 7 Discussion and Conclusion
sections.content: Implications for KV cache quantization. We show that large-Range value states are not only critical for maintaining KV cache eviction accuracy but also major source of error under quantization. Therefore, promising future direction is to investigate mixed-precision approach (Liu et al., 2024b) to maintain the precision of large-Range value states. For example, they can be placed in high-precision reserved cache, while the remaining KV cache is quantized to lower bit-width. Once 9 the high-precision budget is used up, the earliest value state in this reserved cache can be quantized and evicted into the low-precision cache. Large-magnitude value states are crucial for reasoning progression. Our observation in Figure 9 suggests that large-magnitude value states play special role in maintaining reasoning progression. After these values are evicted, generations often degenerate into repetitive loops, where the model repeatedly restates intermediate reasoning without making progress. One hypothesis is that these values help transition between latent reasoning steps, preventing the model from collapsing into self-reinforcing paths. Therefore, their removal traps the model in repetitive loops. One potential future direction is to analyze what information large-magnitude value states carry and how they influence long-form reasoning. Conclusion. We present VASE, training-free KV cache eviction framework designed to address the memory bottleneck of reasoning models. We identify two critical factors for maintaining accuracy during KV cache compression: protecting large-magnitude value states from eviction, and introducing stochasticity to improve KV cache diversity. Taking both factors into consideration, VASE provides simple and effective recipe for improving existing eviction methods. More broadly, our findings highlight the importance of value states in long-form reasoning and suggest new directions for designing memory-efficient inference methods.

sections.content: Variants for Capturing Value-State Magnitude and Variety . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Token Statistics and Hyperparameters . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Broader Impacts . . . . . . . . . . . . . 15 16 17 18 19 19 19 19 Variants for Capturing Value-State Magnitude and Variety Figure 7: Layer-wise violin plots of L2(vi) := vi2, where the value vectors vi are from the full KV cache of Qwen3-4B during GSM8K generation. The distribution shows outliers that have large L2 norm. Figure 8: Layer-wise violin plots of Range(vi), where the values vi are from the full KV cache of Qwen3-4B during GSM8K generation. The distribution shows outliers that have large Range. In this section, we explore different ways to compute the magnitude and variety of value state Rd in KV cache: (1) L2(v) = j=1(vj)2, (2) Range(v) := maxj[d] vj minj[d] vj, and (3) Var(v) = 1 j=1(vj µ)2, where µ = 1 First, we extract the value vectors from the full KV cache during the generation of Qwen3-4B on GSM8K. Figures 7 and 8 present the layer-wise distributions of L2(v) and Range(v), respectively. (cid:113)(cid:80)d j=1 vj. (cid:80)d (cid:80)d 15 The violin plots show prominent outliers that have large L2(v) and Range(v) in different layers. We also compute the Pearson correlation between L2(v) and Range(v) and found that the two are highly correlated (> 0.8) over layers. Next, we study whether preventing these outlier value states from eviction can maintain the accuracy under KV cache compression. We apply the framework introduced in Section 3 that pre-allocates dedicated value slots in the token budget. To decide which entries to keep in the slots, we experiment with the three value-scoring variants: keeping values with the largest L2(v), Range(v), or Var(v), respectively.5 For the remaining budget, we apply stochastic sampling on top of the attention scores in SnapKV as described in Section 3. In Table 3, we compare the value-scoring variants on GSM8K and AIME25. The three variants demonstrate comparable accuracy gains over the SnapKV (attention-only) baseline. Method GSM8K AIME25 SnapKV + L2(v) + Range(v) + Var(v) 64.25 84.55 84.45 84.00 45. 59.17 59.19 57.71 Table 3: Impact of value-state scoring variants on KV cache eviction accuracy. We report the results on Qwen3-4B at 4 compression. The three variants , which capture different aspects of value-state magnitude and variety, achieve comparable performance gains over the SnapKV baseline. We choose Range(v) as the value scoring function for our main approach due to its connection with quantization; specifically, Range(v) captures the extreme values max and min in the vector, which is associated with the outlier challenge in LLM quantization (Dettmers et al., 2022). We show the importance of large-Range value states in Section 3.1, where evicting them causes the model to enter nonsensical reasoning loop:

sections.title: B Token Statistics and Hyperparameters
sections.content: Prompt Tokens Gen Tokens AIME25 AIME26 HMMT25 MATH LiveCodeBench-v6-medium 198 148 127 94 557 17,790 16,642 18,189 5,281 11,088 Table 4: The average token counts of prompt (prefill) and generation (decode) of reasoning tasks. The Gen Tokens column represents the average number of tokens generated by the full Qwen3-4B model. The decode phase yields significantly higher number of tokens than the prefill phase. Table 4 lists the average token counts of the prefill and decode phase of Qwen3-4B, respectively, showing that reasoning models may generate over 10,000 tokens at decode steps for math question that has fewer than 200 prefill tokens. This intensifies the memory-bound constraints of the decode phase, motivating our approach to bound KV cache memory usage to fixed cost. Based on the statistics of Table 4, we set the token budget = 4096 for AIME25, AIME26, and HMMT25, = 1024 for MATH, and = 2048 for GPQA-Diamond, which roughly translates to 4 KV cache compression. For LiveCodeBench, we set = 2048, which is 5 compression. All the eviction methods are training-free but have hyperparameters. We choose the best hyperparameters for each method on GSM8K and then apply them to our main experiments (Section 4.2) without further tuning. For R-KV, we experiment with different redundancy hyperparameters, λ = {0.1, 0.5, 0.9}, and set λ = 0.5. We set the Gaussian matrix rank = 20 for CurDKV and VASE-DKV, and the value reservation budget Nv = K/4 for VASE-ATTNV. 5Here, Range is the same as VASE-ATTNV in the main content. 16 Figure 9: Examples of model outputs on GSM8K when evicting large-Range value states. In the first example, the model starts with reasonable thinking traces but falls into repetitive loop, never reaching the correct answer. In the second example, the model generates the correct answer once (18), but then gets stuck in an endless loop of self-reflection and fails to reach conclusion.

sections.title: MATH
sections.content: 54.6 7.4 R-KV 53.8 7.6 CurDKV 63.3 7.5 VASE-DKV VASE-ATTNV 64.0 7.3 60.2 7.4 61.3 7.3 68.8 6.9 72.3 6.5 44.6 5.7 39.6 5.3 49.4 5.4 50.0 5.6 59.1 3.0 48.5 3.1 56.8 3.0 57.4 3.0 86.1 1.3 75.4 1.7 86.5 1.3 85.4 1.4 Table 5: Reasoning-task accuracy (%) with standard errors for KV cache eviction methods on Qwen314B under 4 cache compression. Table 5 shows the average accuracy standard errors for KV cache eviction methods on Qwen314B under 4 cache compression. For each problem indexed {1, . . . , S}, we sample independent generations and compute the per-problem pass@1 accuracy: ps = 1 (cid:88) r=1 1[sample of problem is correct], (7) where = 16 for datasets with fewer than 100 examples and = 8 otherwise. The overall pass@1 accuracy and its standard error SE are then: = 1 (cid:88) s= ps, SD = (cid:118) (cid:117) (cid:117) (cid:116) 1 1 (cid:88) s= (ps p)2, SE = SD . (8) The variability captured by the standard errors reflects both the stochasticity of the models sampling procedure and, for VASE methods, the randomness introduced by stochastic eviction. On tasks with larger standard errors (e.g., AIME25, AIME26), the small number of test problems (30 each) is the primary cause. While individual confidence intervals overlap due to small test sets, VASE outperforms all baselines across every taskmodel combination. 17 Additional Results Recall that in Figure 3, we demonstrate that reserving Nv slots for large-range values greatly improves GSM8K accuracy. To verify the importance of these specific value states, we conduct an ablation study where we instead reserve Nv slots for randomly sampled KV pairs. Experimenting with Nv = {16, 32, 64} yield accuracies of 65.6%, 63.4%, and 65.3%, respectively. These results are comparable to SnapKV (64.3%) but substantially underperform the green bars in Figure 3, confirming that replacing large-range values with random KV pairs is ineffective. Table 6 shows the Qwen3-14B results of GPQA-Diamond under different token budgets. While R-KV outperforms our VASE methods at 2048 token budget, the accuracy gap closes at 4096 tokens ( 2 compression). In this setting, both R-KV and our methods nearly recover the performance of the full model. Token Budget Compression 2048

sections.content: 4096 2 63.32 60.54 63.38 63.07 Full 1 65.25 Table 6: GPQA-D accuracy for eviction methods on Qwen3-14B under different token budgets. In Figure 10, we extract the full value cache during the generation of Qwen3-4B on GSM8K examples and plot the distributions of Range(v) over different chunks of consecutive tokens. We put the first four sink tokens (Su and Yuan, 2025) into an individual chunk, which shows distinct distributions compared to the other chunks. Except for the first layer (Layer 0), the sink tokens have lower median Range(v), which corresponds to the value-state drain phenomena (Guo et al., 2024). Excluding the sink tokens, the distributions of Range(v) are consistent as the sequence length increases. Figure 10: Range(v) over token position chunks, where each chunk consists of the value states of consecutive tokens. Boxplots illustrate the dynamic range (y-axis) of value states at specific layers. Token positions (x-axis) are bucketed to show how the range distribution evolves through the sequence. The first chunk contains sink tokens at position (0, 4). Excluding the sink tokens, the distribution of Range(v) does not drift as the context window grows. In Figure 11, we show the peak memory footprint of different methods across three KV-cache budgets {2048, 4096, 6144} at 16K output tokens. Overall, VASE-DKV< VASE-ATTNV< R-KV< Full, showing that our VASE methods are more memory-efficient than the R-KV baseline. At 32K output tokens, the memory footprints of different eviction methods remain the same under the same token budgets; however, the Full method can no longer fit single GPU with 80GB memory. 18 Figure 11: Peak GPU memory of Qwen3-14B at 16K output length across three KV-cache budgets {2048, 4096, 6144}. VASE-DKV is the most memory-efficient method at every budget. Limitations Our evaluation focuses on reasoning models and the decode phase of generation, where eviction methods offer the greatest benefit. As result, we do not evaluate on prefill-phase compression benchmarks. However, because our method is built on fundamental observations of value-state distributions and stochastic diversity, we believe the core methodology can be extended to the prefill phase for long-prompt compression. We only implement our methods on Qwen3 models because our selection-based baseline SeerAttention-R only releases checkpoints for Qwen models. Nevertheless, since our value-scoring function relies on simple min and max statistics of the value cache, our method is model-agnostic and can be easily extended to other LLM architectures. Lastly, while our findings link value-state magnitude to quantization errors, we focus on sparse attention, and quantization method development is beyond the scope of this paper. Future research could explore an integrated framework that combines KV cache eviction with outlier-aware quantization to push the limits of compression ratio. Compute Resources Our experiments were conducted on NVIDIA A100 and H100 GPUs. Every experiment can be run on single GPU with 80GB memory. Without PagedAttention and continuous batching, each task takes 10-72 GPU hours, depending on the dataset size and the average number of generated tokens. Licenses As shown in Table 7, we include licenses for any artifacts used in this work. Copyright MAA indicates that AIME problems are copyrighted by the Mathematical Association of America and are used here for evaluation purposes only. Broader Impacts This work proposes training-free KV cache eviction method that reduces the memory footprint of reasoning models during inference. The primary positive impact is enabling more efficient deployment of large language models, reducing hardware requirements and energy consumption. This can broaden access to reasoning-capable models for resource-constrained practitioners and organizations. As general-purpose inference optimization, VASE does not introduce new model capabilities or alter model outputs beyond the approximation inherent in cache compression. It therefore does not directly raise new ethical concerns beyond those already associated with the underlying language models.
